title: Governing Cyber
subtitle: Frameworks, the board & the human firewall

hook.metaphor: A castle's defence isn't really about any single wall — it's about the lord who decides defence matters, the master plan everyone follows, and a garrison trained so well that every guard is a sentry. Cyber maturity is the same: it's a governed program, led from the top, following a recognised playbook, with every employee as part of the wall.
hook.scenario: After a breach, a board asks the CISO: 'Are we secure now?' The honest answer isn't 'yes' — security is never finished. The mature answer reframes the whole question. What should the CISO say instead?

learn: Cyber can't be improvised, so organisations adopt recognised **frameworks** as their playbook: the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover — a clean way to organise everything in this track), **ISO 27001** (a certifiable information-security management system), and Australia's practical **Essential Eight** baseline. Frameworks turn 'are we secure?' into 'how mature are we against a known standard?'

Because perfect security is impossible, mature firms practise **cyber risk management**, not absolutism: identify the crown-jewel assets, assess the risks, and invest proportionately — accepting, reducing, or transferring risk (including **cyber insurance**) within a defined **risk appetite** (Operational Risk Level 6, again).

It must be **governed from the top**: board oversight, a clear owner (often a **CISO**), regular reporting in business terms (not jargon), and cyber treated as an enterprise risk on the same table as credit and operational risk. Regulators increasingly *expect* board-level cyber accountability.

And the most cost-effective control of all: the **human firewall**. Since most attacks target people, ongoing awareness training, phishing simulations, and a 'pause and verify' culture turn the workforce from the weakest link into a distributed sensor network. Culture — tone at the top — is the golden thread here too.

The mature answer to 'are we secure?' is therefore: *'Security is never done. Here's our maturity against the framework, our biggest residual risks, and what we're investing to reduce them.'* That's governance talking, not false comfort.
bridge: So at work, you can lift cyber from a tech checklist to governance: anchor it to a recognised **framework**, treat it as **enterprise risk** within an appetite, demand **board-level ownership and plain-language reporting**, and invest in the **human firewall**. And you can replace the dangerous question 'are we secure?' with the honest one: 'how mature are we, and what are our biggest residual risks?'

interactions.title: 'Are we secure now?'
interactions.prompt: Post-breach, the board asks the CISO if they're secure now. The mature answer?

options.label: 'Yes — we've fixed it, we're secure.'
options.feedback: Declaring 'secure' is false comfort that sets up the next nasty surprise. Security is never finished; absolute safety isn't a thing in cyber.

options.label: 'Security is never done — here's our maturity vs the framework and our top residual risks.'
options.feedback: Exactly. That reframes the question into governance: a standard to measure against, honest residual risks, and a plan. Mature, defensible, and true.

options.label: 'That's an IT question, not for the board.'
options.feedback: The opposite of mature. Cyber is an enterprise risk the board must own and oversee — pushing it back to IT is exactly the failure regulators target.

interactions.title: Governance toolkit
interactions.prompt: Match each concept to its role in governing cyber.

pairs.right: A framework: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover
The senior person accountable for cyber
Transferring some financial risk of an incident
A trained, alert workforce as a frontline defence

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Mature cyber is governed — a recognised framework, board ownership, risk-based investment within appetite, and a trained 'human firewall' — and the honest question is never 'are we secure?' but 'how mature are we, and what are our biggest residual risks?'”

checkpoint.q: Adopting a framework like the NIST CSF or ISO 27001 helps because it…
options: Guarantees you can never be hacked
Turns 'are we secure?' into 'how mature are we against a known standard?'
Replaces the need for any controls
checkpoint.explain: Frameworks give a structured, measurable way to organise and benchmark cyber defence.

checkpoint.q: Treating the workforce as a 'human firewall' means…
options: Training and a verify-first culture so staff become a frontline defence
checkpoint.explain: Since attacks target people, an aware, sceptical workforce is one of the most cost-effective controls.

checkpoint.q: The mature view of cyber security is that it is…
options: A finished state you reach and keep
Never 'done' — managed continuously against residual risk and a standard
checkpoint.explain: Perfect security doesn't exist; you govern it continuously, measuring maturity and managing residual risk.
